steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: biliary colic / choledocholithiasis, ovarian pathology (residual ovarian tissue given partial hysterectomy / fibroid remnant), mesenteric adenitis
ensemble.information_gap: Whether the RLQ pain has a biliary, hepatic, or gynecologic structural source that cannot be distinguished on exam alone — particularly given the patient's obesity making abdominal exam unreliable ('not clear if distended'), developmental delay limiting history, and the absence of any imaging to date
ensemble.expected_finding: Gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, or gallstones (cholecystitis/biliary colic); alternatively, free fluid, an adnexal mass, or other pelvic/RUQ structural abnormality; a normal study would shift suspicion toward appendicitis or other cause
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: With an unreliable abdominal exam due to obesity and developmental delay, elevated WBC (12.2 K/uL with neutrophilia 76.4%), no prior imaging, and a differential spanning biliary to gynecologic to appendiceal etiologies, ultrasound is a rational, radiation-free first-line imaging step to survey the RUQ/pelvis and narrow the differential.
grounding: Physical examination: 'Obese. Well-healed laparoscopy scars. Nontender. Not clear if distended.'
Physical examination: 'obese woman, delayed affect, defers questions to her mother'
Laboratory tests: 'White Blood Cells: 12.2 K/uL (ref 4.0-10.0)'
Laboratory tests: 'Neutrophils: 76.4 % (ref 34.0-71.0)'
Laboratory tests: 'Absolute Neutrophil Count: 9.34 K/uL (ref 1.6-6.1)'
Laboratory tests: 'Lipase: 12.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)' — pancreatitis effectively excluded
Laboratory tests: 'Human Chorionic Gonadotropin: <5' — pregnancy excluded
History: 'PSH: Laparoscopic partial hysterectomy for fibroids' — residual ovarian tissue possible
History: '24 hours of RLQ abd pain with associated nausea and vomiting'
History: 'DM HTN HL Obesity Uterine fibroids'
ensemble.reasoning: This obese woman with developmental delay has an unreliable abdominal exam, leaving the source of her RLQ pain and leukocytosis (WBC 12.2, neutrophilia) anatomically undefined. Pancreatitis is excluded by a normal lipase and pregnancy by a negative hCG, but biliary disease, appendicitis, and residual gynecologic pathology (given prior partial hysterectomy) all remain plausible. Ultrasound is the logical first imaging step: it is radiation-free, can rapidly evaluate the gallbladder/biliary tree and right upper quadrant, and may also visualize pelvic structures, thereby either identifying a treatable biliary source or redirecting workup toward CT for appendicitis.

Pancreatitis is excluded by a normal lipase and pregnancy by a negative hCG, but biliary disease, appendicitis, and residual gynecologic pathology (given prior partial hysterectomy) all remain plausible. Ultrasound is the logical first imaging step: it is radiation-free, can rapidly evaluate the gallbladder/biliary tree and right upper quadrant, and may also visualize pelvic structures, thereby either identifying a treatable biliary source or redirecting workup toward CT for appendicitis.

verification.actual_finding: The ultrasound showed a normal gallbladder with no stones, no wall thickening, no pericholecystic fluid, normal bile ducts, normal liver, and no ascites.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The study was negative for all biliary and hepatic structural abnormalities the physician was hoping to identify, and no adnexal or free fluid findings were reported, shifting suspicion away from a biliary/hepatic source toward appendicitis or another cause as the physician anticipated.

steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: gynecologic pathology (ovarian torsion, tubo-ovarian abscess, adnexal mass given history of uterine fibroids/partial hysterectomy), bowel obstruction, mesenteric adenitis, or other intra-abdominal process
ensemble.information_gap: The source of RLQ pain remains unidentified after ultrasound: the appendix was not visualized, pelvic/adnexal structures were not assessed, and the colon/small bowel were not evaluated — leaving the anatomic cause of pain entirely uncertain.
ensemble.expected_finding: Periappendiceal fat stranding with a dilated, non-compressible appendix (appendicitis), or alternatively adnexal pathology, colonic wall thickening (diverticulitis), or another intra-abdominal inflammatory/obstructive process explaining the RLQ pain and leukocytosis.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The ultrasound did not visualize the appendix or pelvic structures, the patient has RLQ pain with leukocytosis (WBC 12.2, neutrophilia 76.4%, ANC 9.34) and a low-grade fever, and CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the appropriate next step to identify the anatomic source of pain that ultrasound could not characterize in this obese patient.
grounding: History: '24 hours of RLQ abd pain with associated nausea and vomiting'
Vitals: '99.7 110 130/70 18 98%ra' — low-grade fever and tachycardia
Labs: 'White Blood Cells: 12.2 K/uL (ref 4.0-10.0)' — leukocytosis
Labs: 'Neutrophils: 76.4 % (ref 34.0-71.0)' and 'Absolute Neutrophil Count: 9.34 K/uL' — neutrophilia
Labs: 'Human Chorionic Gonadotropin: <5' — pregnancy excluded
Physical exam: 'Obese. Well-healed laparoscopy scars. Nontender. Not clear if distended.' — unreliable exam in obese patient with developmental delay
Prior imaging: gallbladder and liver normal, appendix not mentioned as visualized, pancreas partially obscured, kidneys limited views — ultrasound non-diagnostic for RLQ source
PMH: 'Uterine fibroids' and 'Laparoscopic partial hysterectomy for fibroids' — gynecologic history raises adnexal pathology concern
Labs: 'Lipase: 12.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)' — pancreatitis effectively excluded
ensemble.reasoning: This obese woman with developmental delay presents with 24 hours of RLQ pain, low-grade fever, tachycardia, and leukocytosis with neutrophilia, but the abdominal exam is unreliable and the ultrasound — while ruling out biliary and hepatic pathology — did not visualize the appendix or pelvic structures. With pregnancy excluded and pancreatitis biochemically ruled out, the leading concern is appendicitis versus a gynecologic or colonic process, and CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the most efficient way to localize the source and guide surgical decision-making in a patient whose exam cannot be trusted.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: gynecologic pathology (ovarian torsion, tubo-ovarian abscess, adnexal mass given history of uterine fibroids/partial hysterectomy), bowel obstruction, mesenteric adenitis, or other intra-abdominal process
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: The source of RLQ pain remains unidentified after ultrasound: the appendix was not visualized, pelvic/adnexal structures were not assessed, and the colon/small bowel were not evaluated — leaving the anatomic cause of pain entirely uncertain.
representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: Periappendiceal fat stranding with a dilated, non-compressible appendix (appendicitis), or alternatively adnexal pathology, colonic wall thickening (diverticulitis), or another intra-abdominal inflammatory/obstructive process explaining the RLQ pain and leukocytosis.
representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: The ultrasound did not visualize the appendix or pelvic structures, the patient has RLQ pain with leukocytosis (WBC 12.2, neutrophilia 76.4%, ANC 9.34) and a low-grade fever, and CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the appropriate next step to identify the anatomic source of pain that ultrasound could not characterize in this obese patient.

verification.actual_finding: The appendix is enlarged (up to 1.2 cm), hyperemic, and fluid-filled with severe right lower quadrant periappendiceal fat stranding, compatible with appendicitis, without perforation or drainable collection.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The CT directly identified the expected finding of a dilated, inflamed appendix with periappendiceal fat stranding, fully resolving the anatomic cause of the RLQ pain and leukocytosis.
